titleStmt.title: Letter from Sir Wilfrid Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender, circa June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Wilfrid Spender

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
This letter is part of a series of correspondence from Sir Wilfrid Bliss Spender to his wife Lady Lillian Spender. The letters date mainly from 1915 to 1918, when Sir Wilfrid was serving in France. The letters concern politics, the Irish question, personal life, and Wilfrid's experience of the Great War.
In this letter Spender is writing to his wife, Lady Lillian Spender, née Dean, (1880-1968). Sir Wilfrid Spender (1876 - 1960) was a member of the British Army until 1913 when controversy surrounding his signature of the Ulster Solemn League and Covenant resulted in his resignation. Spender was called up for military service in 1914 and from 1915 served in the 36th (Ulster) Division. Throughout the war, Spender remained in contact with the Ulster Unionist leadership, and consistently opposed Home Rule and the six-county solution.
In this letter, Spender tells his wife of life at the front, asks her to find a watchmaker to fix his broken watch, and regrets that he has had no news from his brother Rex.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
D1633/1/1/303
I would if could be the Ulsters but not
if G N & P remain. Comyn in saying
goodbye said he found that relations
between G & Q would become more &
more strained now I had gone, &
added that their would have been
several rows already if he hadn't
been afraid of hurting my feelings.
This was rather nice but it makes
me sad & anxious for the future. Douglas has got the school as
his billet at night which is rather
lucky & with camp bed he
does very well. M Gill turned up
with both horns last night but I
have not yet seen him. If you find yourself near the
watchmaker's could you send me
out another cover to my watch as
I have broken mine. I wish I could get news of Rex
& also of other people. No doubt I
have been a bit spoilt in the past I had a talk with Gen Harper the other
day who says Alfred Spinder is now very
subdued & can raise no defence on either
the Irish or German questions. He must
have changed lately Don't forget Rex's weekly parcel
please If it is a lot of trouble give a
standing order for 5/- a week
Your rather forlorn questionable reading
W B Spender.
